title: Observable proof lifecycle with typed event emitters
description: Add a typed EventEmitter to the SDK's prove/verify methods so developers can observe progress (witness-generating, proving, verifying) and surface UX feedback. Currently proveHandshake() is a black-box async call that can take 2-30 seconds depending on snarkjs vs rapidsnark — developers have no way to show progress indicators. Expose events like 'witness:start', 'proving:start', 'proving:complete' with elapsed-time metadata. This is critical for framework integrations (LangChain, CrewAI) where tool timeouts need calibration.

title: Fluent credential builder with compile-time permission validation
description: Replace the positional-argument createAgentCredential(modelHash, operatorPrivKey, permissions, expiry) with a builder pattern: Bolyra.credential().model('gpt-4o').operator(key).permit('FINANCIAL_SMALL', 'READ_DATA').expiresIn('24h').build(). The builder enforces cumulative-bit implication rules at construction time (TypeScript type narrowing + runtime check), so developers never hit the cryptic circuit-level 'cumulative encoding violation' error. Current API requires knowing the raw bitmask encoding and manually setting implied bits.

title: Structured error taxonomy with recovery hints
description: Define a BolyraError class hierarchy with machine-readable codes (STALE_ROOT, EXPIRED_CREDENTIAL, SCOPE_MISMATCH, NULLIFIER_REUSED, PROOF_GENERATION_FAILED) and a .recovery property suggesting what the developer should do. Currently snarkjs failures surface as opaque 'Error: Assert Failed' or raw Solidity reverts. Map every circuit constraint failure and contract revert to a named error with a one-line fix suggestion. Ship as @bolyra/errors re-exported from the main SDK.

title: Dry-run mode: witness-only validation without proving
description: Add a { dryRun: true } option to proveHandshake() and delegate() that runs witness generation only (the 'fast' path from test:circuits:fast) and returns a WitnessResult with computed public signals but no proof. This lets developers validate inputs in <100ms during development instead of waiting for full Groth16 proving. The test suite already splits fast/slow — expose this split as a first-class SDK concept so integration tests in CI run 50x faster.

title: SessionManager abstraction for nonce lifecycle
description: Introduce a SessionManager class that owns the nonce lifecycle: generate, bind to proofs, track used nonces, and auto-refresh on expiry. Currently developers must manually generate sessionNonce, pass it to both human and agent prove calls, then track it for replay protection. The SessionManager would expose session.start() → { nonce, proveHuman(), proveAgent(), verifyHandshake() } with the nonce pre-bound, eliminating the most common integration mistake (nonce mismatch between human and agent proofs).

title: Off-chain JWT session token from verified handshake
description: After a successful on-chain handshake verification, emit a short-lived JWT (HS256, 5-min TTL) containing the nullifierHash, scopeCommitment, and sessionNonce as claims. LangChain/CrewAI agents can pass this JWT as a Bearer token on subsequent tool calls without re-proving. The SDK should expose `handshakeToJWT(handshakeResult, signingSecret)` and a `verifyBolyraJWT(token, signingSecret)` middleware. This eliminates the per-call proving overhead that makes framework adoption impractical for multi-step agent chains.

title: LangChain RunnableMiddleware for automatic Bolyra auth injection
description: Build a LangChain `RunnablePassthrough`-style middleware that wraps any tool chain and automatically injects Bolyra proof metadata into the chain's context. Instead of requiring developers to manually call `proveHandshake()` before each tool, the middleware intercepts the first invocation, triggers proving, caches the session token, and attaches it to all subsequent calls in the chain. Ship as `@bolyra/langchain` with a 3-line integration: `chain = bolyra_auth(agent_cred) | existing_chain`. Targets the 80% case where developers abandon ZK auth because it requires restructuring their existing chains.

title: CrewAI task delegation mapped to Bolyra scope narrowing
description: CrewAI's `Task(agent=researcher).delegate(agent=writer)` has no permission model — any agent can delegate any task. Build a `BolyraCrewAI` adapter that maps CrewAI role assignments to Bolyra permission bitmasks and automatically generates a Delegation proof when `Task.delegate()` is called. The delegatee's scope is the intersection of the delegator's permissions and the task's required permissions (declared via a `required_permissions` field on `Task`). This gives CrewAI users cryptographic enforcement of least-privilege without changing their workflow.

title: Standardized proof envelope with application/bolyra+json content type
description: Define a canonical JSON envelope for Bolyra proofs that framework integrations can produce and consume uniformly: `{version, proofType, proof: {pi_a, pi_b, pi_c}, publicSignals, metadata: {proverVersion, provingTimeMs}}`. Register `application/bolyra+json` as the content type. LangChain tools, CrewAI tasks, and AutoGen agents all need a common wire format to pass proofs between heterogeneous frameworks. Without this, each integration invents its own serialization and they can't interoperate in multi-framework pipelines.

title: AutoGen GroupChat auth gate with per-agent scope enforcement
description: AutoGen's GroupChat pattern lets multiple agents collaborate in a shared conversation. Build an `AuthenticatedGroupChat` subclass where each agent must present a valid Bolyra AgentPolicy proof before joining. The GroupChat manager verifies proofs and enforces that agents can only invoke tools matching their permission bitmask. Messages from agents whose scope doesn't include the required permission bits are rejected with a structured error. This addresses the AutoGen community's top security concern: any agent in a GroupChat can currently call any tool.

title: Cross-chain Merkle root sync via EIP-1186 storage proofs
description: Deploy IdentityRegistry on Arbitrum and Polygon alongside the existing Base Sepolia deployment. Implement a RootRelay contract that accepts EIP-1186 storage proofs of the humanTree and agentTree roots from the canonical chain (Base), verified against the L1 state root available via each L2's precompile (ArbSys on Arbitrum, StateSender on Polygon). This eliminates trusted relayers — any user can permissionlessly sync roots cross-chain with a single tx containing the storage proof.

title: Chain-scoped nullifier domain separation
description: The current nullifierHash in HumanUniqueness is Poseidon2(scope, secret) and in AgentPolicy is Poseidon2(credentialCommitment, sessionNonce). Neither includes a chain identifier, so a valid proof on Base can be replayed on Arbitrum or Polygon if the same IdentityRegistry is deployed with the same roots. Add a public input chainId to all three circuits and bind it into the nullifier: Poseidon3(scope, secret, chainId) for humans, Poseidon3(credentialCommitment, sessionNonce, chainId) for agents. The on-chain verifier checks chainId == block.chainid.

title: Unified root history buffer with L2 finality awareness
description: The IdentityRegistry has a 30-root history buffer for agentTree but none for humanTree (noted in seed candidates). In a cross-chain setting, root propagation latency compounds — a proof generated against the Base humanTree root may arrive on Arbitrum 10-15 minutes later. Implement a 30-root ring buffer for humanTree matching agentTree's pattern, and on relay chains extend both buffers to 60 entries to account for cross-chain propagation delay. Add a rootTimestamp mapping so relay contracts can reject roots older than a configurable MAX_ROOT_AGE (default 1 hour).

title: Cross-chain nonce deduplication via shared nullifier set
description: usedNonces and usedDelegationNullifiers are per-chain mappings. An attacker could complete a handshake on Base, then replay the same (humanProof, agentProof, nonce) on Polygon before the nullifier propagates. Implement a NullifierAggregator contract on the canonical chain (Base) that receives nullifier attestations from all deployment chains via their native bridges (Arbitrum Retryable Tickets, Polygon FxPortal). Each IdentityRegistry checks the aggregator before accepting a nonce. For latency tolerance, allow optimistic acceptance with a 30-minute challenge window.

title: Cross-chain test vectors for conformance suite
description: Extend the conformance test suite (seed: conformance-test-suite) with 10+ cross-chain-specific JSON test vectors: valid proof on chain A rejected on chain B due to chainId mismatch, stale relayed root beyond MAX_ROOT_AGE, nullifier replay across chains, storage proof with invalid state root, and delegation chain spanning two chains (hop 1 on Base, hop 2 on Arbitrum). These vectors should be chain-agnostic (parameterized by chainId) so any future deployment can validate interop correctness.

title: Define off-chain session token format for verified handshakes
description: After a successful on-chain handshake verification, there is no standardized bearer token format for subsequent off-chain API calls within the same session. Define a compact JWT-like session token (JWS over the handshake public signals: humanNullifier, agentNullifier, sessionNonce, scopeCommitment) with an IANA-registerable media type `application/bolyra-session+jwt`. This lets relying parties skip on-chain re-verification for the session lifetime while maintaining cryptographic binding to the original ZKP handshake.

title: Standardize proof envelope wire format with IANA content type
description: The SDK currently passes proof objects as ad-hoc JSON blobs with no schema versioning or content negotiation. Define a versioned proof envelope (`application/bolyra-proof+cbor` / `+json`) with fields: `version`, `circuitId`, `provingSystem` (groth16|plonk), `publicSignals[]`, `proof` (π_A, π_B, π_C or equivalent), and `metadata` (chain, registryAddress). Add an ABNF grammar to the IETF draft §4. This is prerequisite for any multi-implementation interop — without it, each SDK reinvents serialization.

title: Complete DID resolution algorithm with verification methods
description: The existing `spec/did-method-bolyra.md` defines the DID syntax but lacks a complete resolution algorithm per W3C DID Core §7.1. Specify: (1) how `did:bolyra:<commitment>` resolves to a DID Document by querying IdentityRegistry events, (2) verification method entries mapping to the EdDSA Baby Jubjub keys (for agents) or the nullifier-based authentication (for humans), (3) service endpoints pointing to the on-chain registry. Without this, no DID resolver library can implement `did:bolyra` support.

title: Publish deterministic JSON test vectors for cross-implementation conformance
description: Create a `spec/test-vectors/` directory with 30+ JSON files covering: valid handshake round-trip, expired agent credential rejection, stale Merkle root, scope subset violation, cumulative bit encoding violations (bit 4 without 3, bit 3 without 2), delegation chain at max depth, and nullifier replay. Each vector includes deterministic inputs (using a fixed secret/key pair), expected intermediate values (commitment hashes), and expected pass/fail outcome. Reference these from the IETF draft §A (Appendix). This is the single most impactful interop artifact — any alternative SDK implementation can validate correctness against these vectors.

title: Specify human tree root history buffer matching agent tree parity
description: IdentityRegistry maintains a 30-root history buffer for agentTree but has no equivalent for humanTree — the contract comment says 'humanTree uses Semaphore's' but the code uses a standalone LeanIMT with no history. This means any new human enrollment instantly invalidates all in-flight human proofs. Add a `humanRootHistory[30]` ring buffer with the same `isKnownHumanRoot()` check pattern used by `isKnownAgentRoot()`. Document the staleness window (30 enrollments ≈ N minutes at expected throughput) in the IETF draft Security Considerations §7.

title: Prove BinaryMerkleRoot depth parameter cannot be manipulated to accept invalid proofs
description: The `merkleProofLength` input in all three circuits controls how many siblings are actually used by BinaryMerkleRoot, but there is no constraint that `merkleProofLength` matches the expected tree depth. An attacker could supply a shorter proof path against a subtree root that happens to be in the root history buffer. Write a formal property (Certora-style invariant or circom assert) proving that for any accepted witness, the Merkle root output corresponds to a tree of exactly the deployed depth, or add an equality constraint `merkleProofLength === EXPECTED_DEPTH` to each circuit.

title: Prove nullifier domain separation between Human, Agent, and Delegation circuits
description: HumanUniqueness computes `nullifier = Poseidon2(scope, secret)`, AgentPolicy computes `nullifier = Poseidon2(credentialCommitment, sessionNonce)`, and Delegation computes its own nullifier. If the Poseidon input domains overlap (e.g., a `scope` value equals a `credentialCommitment`), cross-circuit nullifier collisions could cause false replay rejections or, worse, allow a human nullifier to block an agent. Add explicit domain-separation tags (e.g., prepend a circuit-specific constant) to each nullifier hash and write a formal proof that no two circuits can produce the same nullifier for any valid inputs.

title: Verify cumulative bit encoding is both sound and complete in Delegation circuit
description: The Delegation circuit enforces that bit 4 implies bits 2+3 and bit 3 implies bit 2 on the delegatee's scope. Write exhaustive formal properties proving: (1) soundness — no witness with a violating bitmask can satisfy the constraints, and (2) completeness — all 256 valid 8-bit patterns that satisfy the implication rules are accepted. Currently only the delegatee side is checked; verify that the delegator's scope also satisfies cumulative encoding, since an invalid delegator scope could let an attacker bypass the subset check.

title: Prove scopeCommitment uniquely binds (scope, identity) across delegation chain
description: The delegation chain links hops via `scopeCommitment = Poseidon2(scope, credCommitment)`. If two distinct (scope, credCommitment) pairs collide under Poseidon2, an attacker could splice delegation chains. Write a formal argument (collision resistance under the algebraic group model) and a concrete test harness that fuzzes 10M random input pairs to empirically verify no collisions in the BN254 field. Also verify that the on-chain `previousScopeCommitment` check in IdentityRegistry correctly rejects chain-splicing attempts.

title: Prove expiry comparisons are safe against field arithmetic overflow
description: AgentPolicy checks `currentTimestamp < expiryTimestamp` using a LessThan(64) comparator, and Delegation checks `delegateeExpiry <= delegatorExpiry`. Both rely on Num2Bits(64) range checks to prevent field-element overflow. Write a formal proof that: (1) any expiryTimestamp ≥ 2^64 is rejected by the range check before reaching the comparator, (2) the LessThan(64) comparator is correct for all uint64 pairs (no off-by-one at boundaries 0 and 2^64-1), and (3) the Solidity-side timestamp freshness check (`DelegationTimestampStale` / `AgentTimestampStale`) cannot be bypassed by choosing a currentTimestamp that passes the circuit but is stale on-chain.

title: Replace EdDSA verification with Poseidon-based credential binding in AgentPolicy
description: The AgentPolicy circuit spends ~30k constraints on EdDSAPoseidonVerifier to check the operator signature over the credential commitment. Since the credential commitment is already proven to be in the Merkle tree (which the operator populated at enrollment time), the EdDSA check is redundant for proving authorization — the Merkle inclusion already proves the operator enrolled this exact credential. Removing the in-circuit EdDSA verification and instead checking the signature off-chain before proof generation would cut constraint count from ~45-50k to ~15-20k, yielding a 2-3x proving speed improvement. The operator signature is still verified, just moved to the SDK layer before witness generation.

title: Deduplicate Poseidon round constants across ModelInstanceBinding's dual EdDSA checks
description: ModelInstanceBinding instantiates two full EdDSAPoseidonVerifier components (operator + provider), each inlining their own Poseidon round constants and S-box chain. Since both use identical Poseidon parameters, extracting a shared round-constant template and wiring both verifiers to the same constant signals would eliminate ~3,000 redundant constraints from the ~35k total. This is a mechanical refactor: define a PoseidonConstants template, instantiate once, and feed both EdDSA verifiers from it.

title: Narrow LessThan bit-width in Delegation expiry check from 64 to 40 bits
description: The Delegation circuit uses LessThan(64) for the delegateeExpiry <= delegatorExpiry check, costing 128 constraints. Unix timestamps won't exceed 2^40 until the year 36,812. Using LessThan(40) saves 48 constraints per comparison and, more importantly, tightens the valid range — a 64-bit LessThan allows field-element values that look valid in the circuit but overflow uint64 on the Solidity side despite the Num2Bits(64) range check (since Num2Bits and LessThan operate on independent decompositions). Adding a shared Num2Bits(40) for both timestamps and feeding those bits to LessThan(40) eliminates this class of subtle mismatch.

title: Cache BabyPbk scalar multiplication via public-key-as-private-input pattern
description: HumanUniqueness spends ~5,000+ constraints on BabyPbk (fixed-base scalar multiplication) to derive (Ax, Ay) from the secret. An alternative pattern used by Semaphore v4 itself: take (Ax, Ay) as private inputs, verify Poseidon2(Ax, Ay) matches the Merkle leaf (already done), and derive the nullifier as Poseidon2(scope, secret) (already done). The scalar-mul is only needed to bind secret→pubkey, but since the Merkle tree already binds pubkey→enrollment, the binding chain secret→nullifier + pubkey→tree is sufficient if we add a single constraint: Poseidon3(secret, Ax, Ay) checked against a commitment stored at enrollment. This cuts ~5k constraints, bringing HumanUniqueness from ~20k to ~15k and improving mobile proving time by ~25%.

title: Consolidate cumulative-bit encoding checks into single algebraic expression
description: Both AgentPolicy and Delegation enforce cumulative bit encoding (bit4→bits2+3, bit3→bit2) using separate IsEqual/comparator components per implication rule, each instantiating Num2Bits decompositions. These three implication constraints can be expressed as a single algebraic identity: bit4*(1-bit2) === 0, bit4*(1-bit3) === 0, bit3*(1-bit2) === 0 — three raw quadratic constraints with zero additional components, reusing the bits already decomposed by the Num2Bits(64) range check. This eliminates ~200 constraints worth of comparator overhead per circuit and makes the invariant easier to formally verify since it's pure R1CS.

title: Off-chain session token (JWT) after on-chain handshake
description: After a successful on-chain handshake verification, emit a signed JWT session token that encodes the verified scope, nullifiers, and expiry. Subsequent API calls within the session present this JWT instead of re-proving on-chain. This is the critical path to production adoption: no agentic commerce platform will pay gas per API call. Implement a SessionTokenIssuer in the SDK that takes handshake proof outputs and returns a compact JWT, plus a verifySessionToken() function that checks signature, expiry, and scope without touching the chain. Add a spec section defining the JWT claims (scope_commitment, human_nullifier, agent_nullifier, session_nonce, iat, exp).

title: Agent credential rotation without service interruption
description: Production agents need key rotation (compromised keys, expiring certs, operator changes). Currently there's no path to rotate an agent's credential without re-enrollment and invalidating all active delegation chains. Add a rotateAgentCredential(oldCommitment, newCredential) to IdentityRegistry that atomically replaces the leaf in agentTree, preserving the agent's position. Expose SDK method rotateCredential() that generates the new commitment, calls the contract, and returns updated credential material. Without this, any operator key compromise requires a full re-enrollment cascade across all delegatees — a non-starter for enterprise adoption.

title: Payment-bound handshake for agentic commerce
description: Extend the handshake protocol with an optional payment commitment: the agent's proof includes a hash of (amount, currency, recipient, deadline) as an additional public signal. The verifier contract checks that the payment commitment matches an escrow deposit or a pre-authorized ERC-20 allowance before accepting the handshake. This makes Bolyra the identity layer for agent-to-agent payments — the 10-year endgame. Implement as a PaymentBoundRegistry contract that wraps IdentityRegistry.verifyHandshake() with escrow logic, plus SDK helpers createPaymentCommitment() and settlePayment(). Start with USDC on Base.

title: Hosted proving API for zero-setup integration
description: The single biggest adoption blocker is that developers must compile circuits, download ptau files, and manage rapidsnark binaries locally. Build a hosted proving API (POST /prove/handshake, POST /prove/agent-policy) that accepts witness inputs over HTTPS and returns the proof. Deploy as a stateless Lambda/Cloud Run behind an API key. The SDK gets a new constructor option: createBolyraClient({ provingMode: 'hosted', apiKey }) that transparently routes proving through the API. Free tier: 100 proofs/month. This turns integration from 'install circom + snarkjs + rapidsnark' to 'npm install @bolyra/sdk && set API key'.

title: Multi-agent session with aggregate scope tracking
description: Real agentic workflows involve multiple agents collaborating in a single session (e.g., a research agent delegates to a booking agent which delegates to a payment agent). Currently each delegation is tracked independently with no way to query the aggregate permission state of a session. Add a SessionManager contract that tracks all active agents in a session, their individual scope commitments, and the aggregate scope (intersection of all active scopes). Expose getSessionAgents(nonce) and getAggregateScope(nonce) views. SDK gets a Session class: const session = await bolyra.createSession(); session.addAgent(agentProof); session.delegate(from, to, narrowedScope). This is the coordination primitive agentic commerce needs.

title: HumanUniqueness nullifier enables cross-session identity linkage
description: nullifierHash = Poseidon2(scope, secret) with a constant scope (e.g. hash('bolyra-handshake-v1')) produces the same nullifier every time the same human authenticates. Any observer collecting handshake transcripts can link all sessions to the same identity, breaking the privacy guarantee that ZKP-based auth is supposed to provide. The sessionNonce only binds via nonceBinding output but is NOT mixed into the nullifier. Fix: either rotate scope per-session (nullifier = Poseidon3(scope, secret, sessionNonce)) accepting single-use nullifiers, or introduce an epoch-based nullifier scheme where scope = hash(epoch_counter) so linkability is bounded to epoch windows. Both approaches require updating IdentityRegistry's usedNonces mapping to track nullifiers per-epoch rather than globally.

title: scopeCommitment is brute-forceable due to 8-bit permission space
description: AgentPolicy outputs scopeCommitment = Poseidon2(permissionBitmask, credentialCommitment). Although permissionBitmask is declared as 64-bit, only 8 bits carry meaning (the permission model uses bits 0-7). An observer who knows the credentialCommitment (it's the Merkle leaf, recoverable from enrollment events) can brute-force all 256 valid bitmask values against the public scopeCommitment to determine the agent's exact permissions. This leaks the scope bits that the privacy model (Delegation.circom header) explicitly promises to keep private. Fix: add a random blinding salt as a third Poseidon input — scopeCommitment = Poseidon3(permissionBitmask, credentialCommitment, blindingSalt) — where blindingSalt is a private input sampled fresh per proof.

title: Unconstrained merkleProofLength allows depth-0 root forgery
description: All three circuits pass merkleProofLength as a private signal to BinaryMerkleRoot without range-checking that 1 <= merkleProofLength <= MAX_DEPTH. If merkleProofLength = 0, the BinaryMerkleRoot component returns the leaf itself as the root. An attacker can craft a credential commitment, set depth = 0, and the circuit outputs that commitment as the Merkle root. If the on-chain root history check doesn't catch this (e.g. root history contains a value that happens to match), membership is forged. Even if current roots never match, this is a constraint soundness bug. Fix: add a RangeCheck component asserting merkleProofLength >= 1 via IsZero + assert, and merkleProofLength <= MAX_DEPTH via LessThan(5). Apply to HumanUniqueness, AgentPolicy, and Delegation circuits.

title: No domain separation between human, agent, and delegation nullifiers
description: HumanUniqueness computes nullifierHash = Poseidon2(scope, secret). AgentPolicy computes nullifierHash = Poseidon2(sessionNonce, credentialCommitment) (or similar 2-input Poseidon). Delegation computes delegationNullifier similarly. All three use the same Poseidon arity without a domain-separation tag. If inputs across circuits accidentally collide (e.g. a scope value equals a sessionNonce, and a secret equals a credentialCommitment), the nullifiers collide. On-chain, IdentityRegistry tracks human and agent nullifiers in `usedNonces` — a cross-circuit collision would cause a legitimate proof to be rejected as 'already used'. Fix: prepend a circuit-specific domain tag as the first Poseidon input: Poseidon3(DOMAIN_HUMAN, scope, secret), Poseidon3(DOMAIN_AGENT, sessionNonce, credCmt), Poseidon3(DOMAIN_DELEGATION, ...). Use distinct hardcoded constants for each domain.

title: humanTree has no root history buffer — proofs go stale on any enrollment
description: IdentityRegistry maintains a 30-root history buffer for agentTree (agentRootHistory) but humanTree has no equivalent. When a new human enrolls, the humanTree root changes immediately, and any in-flight HumanUniqueness proof (which commits to the old root via humanMerkleRoot output) becomes invalid. The contract's verifyHandshake checks the proof's root against the current tree root only. In production with concurrent enrollments, this creates a race condition where legitimate proofs are rejected. Fix: add a parallel humanRootHistory[30] ring buffer to IdentityRegistry, update it on every enrollHuman call, and add an isKnownHumanRoot(root) check mirroring the existing isKnownAgentRoot pattern. The HumanUniqueness circuit itself needs no changes — only the on-chain root validation logic.
